purpose: ADR-0007 ratchet applied to the mcp-agent-path concurrent-load SLO. scripts/concurrent_load_gate.py reads THIS committed file for its enforced ceilings instead of hard-coded, env-overridable defaults. The gate fails when a measured statistic reaches its 'ratchet_ms'. 'slo_ms' is the hard product SLO that the ratchet may never be relaxed to or past (the loader asserts ratchet_ms < slo_ms); 'ratchet_ms' is the committed high-water ceiling that turns one way — a change that proves a lower number lowers it here in the same PR, and a change that needs a higher one must raise it here with a justification visible in diff review. Env vars (LOAD_GATE_*_MS) may only TIGHTEN a ceiling for local experiments; they can never loosen it, so no change can silently regress past what is committed here.
notes: Seeded from the real sandbox-CI high-water, not a dev box: measured_high_water_ms below are from the HARDEN-50 public-CI run (8 agents x 20 rounds, projectplanner-ci run 29136993388), which runs ~2-10x slower and noisier than local. ratchet_ms is set ~1.6-2.6x above that CI high-water so ordinary runner variance never trips the gate while a real regression does — the incident this defends against (reintroduced client-visible locks) serializes writes and pushes p99 into the hundreds of ms, far past every ratchet here. all_calls.max is the single worst call and is left deliberately generous (only a spike into the seconds is pathological enough to matter). HARDEN-62 records multi-run CI numbers and tightens each ratchet_ms toward its measured high-water + margin.
